B
Control Grade
82.5/100

Controlled, minor gaps. Primarily capped by change traceability.

heuristic3 of 7 controls measured
Repository: sample-repo

Dimensions — 3 of 7 measurable

OK25%1.00

Requirement traceability

Can you explain why every piece of code exists?

Every change should trace back to a reason you asked for it — and your features should be backed by tests.

In your repo: 1/1 FRs covered; 2/2 changes traced (FR-linked or classified no-FR)

Why it matters

Build fast with AI and code piles up. Six months later you find a function and nobody remembers what it's for, or whether it's safe to delete — and "it has tests" often means a few test files nobody runs. This checks whether each change is tied to a requirement (or honestly marked as maintenance), and whether your features are traced to tests.

With Shipwright, every change is linked to the requirement it serves or explicitly classified as maintenance, and features are traced to their tests — so your repo stays explainable instead of a pile no one dares touch.

Backed by: The change-control discipline banks and insurers get audited on.

How this was measured: route/feature inference + git-log classification (heuristic) · heuristic · @ deadbeefcafe · maps to the standard: requirement-to-work traceability (ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148)

N/A20%N/A

Test health

Do your tests actually run and pass — recently?

Not "are there test files" — did the test suite actually run lately, and pass?

We couldn't measure this from the outside — and that's the point.

Why it matters

A repo can be full of tests nobody has run in months, or a suite that's half-red that everyone ignores. That's false confidence — the worst kind. This checks for a real, recent run where (almost) everything passed, reading your CI's actual test results where they exist.

With Shipwright, the suite runs on every change and the result is recorded with a date — "it's green" becomes a fact, not a hope.

One honest limit: this checks that tests run and pass, not how much of your code they cover — and green tests alone don't prove the whole app works. That deeper check is what Reconciliation adds.

Backed by: OpenSSF Scorecard's "automated tests run" signal — the same check used across ~1M repositories.

How this was measured: static test inventory — present, not executed · unavailable · maps to the standard: automated tests pass (OpenSSF Scorecard)

GAP15%0.50

Change traceability

Can you see who changed what, and why?

Every change should leave a paper trail — a commit, and the reason it happened.

In your repo: 1/2 changes linked to a commit, ADR or test run

Why it matters

AI can make dozens of edits an afternoon. If your history is a wall of "update", "fix", "wip", you can't review it, audit it, or safely undo one thing. This checks whether changes are tied to a commit and a reason (an issue, a task, a requirement).

With Shipwright, every change is recorded like a logbook entry — what changed, why, which requirement, which test — so your history reads like a story, not a mystery. (N/A here just means we couldn't see reviewed-PR provenance from the outside — run it with network access, or adopt to switch it on.)

Backed by: Software supply-chain provenance (SLSA).

How this was measured: git-log PR/issue links (heuristic) · heuristic · @ deadbeefcafe · maps to the standard: change provenance (SLSA)

N/A15%N/A

Change reconciliation

When an AI change touches a feature, is that feature re-checked?

The one control almost nobody has: when a change quietly affects a feature, its tests run again — so side-effects can't slip through unseen.

We couldn't measure this from the outside — and that's the point.

Why it matters

You ask the AI to "fix the login bug." It fixes login — but it also quietly changes how passwords get checked. Did anyone re-test the password check? Usually nobody noticed the side-effect, so nobody re-tested it. That's the bug that ships to your users. This is the biggest risk of building with AI, and almost no tool catches it — because it needs a system that knows which change touched which feature.

That's exactly what Shipwright adds: it flags every behavior-affecting change until that feature is proven to still work. This is why we can't see it from the outside — and why your grade jumps the moment you adopt.

Backed by: How regulated industries like banks and insurers re-verify a requirement whenever a change touches it.

How this was measured: Shipwright-only: per-change behaviour re-verification · unavailable · maps to the standard: re-verify changed requirements (ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207)

N/A10%N/A

Security

Any known-dangerous holes sitting open?

Did a security scan run recently — and come back clean of serious issues?

We couldn't measure this from the outside — and that's the point.

Why it matters

AI happily copies insecure patterns it learned online — a leaked key here, an injectable query there. If nobody scans, you're guessing about your risk. This checks for a recent scan with no open high-severity issues.

With Shipwright, security scans run inside your build and block a change that introduces something critical — "secure" is enforced, not hoped for. (N/A here just means we couldn't see a scan from the outside — run it with network access, or adopt to switch it on.)

Backed by: NIST secure-development guidance / OWASP.

How this was measured: deferred to G2: code-scanning SARIF / local scan · unavailable · maps to the standard: no open high/critical vulns (NIST SSDF)

GAP10%0.85

Size / maintainability discipline

Is the codebase staying sane, or ballooning?

Are files staying a reasonable size, or is the code quietly growing into a mess?

In your repo: 2/13 source files over 300 LOC

Why it matters

AI loves to generate giant files and paste the same logic five times. It works today — but every month it gets harder to change anything without breaking three other things. This checks whether files stay a reasonable size (and, once you adopt, whether each change quietly adds bloat).

With Shipwright, unchecked growth is prevented — code size is tracked on every change and net growth gets flagged — so the codebase stays workable as it grows.

Backed by: ISO 25010 maintainability.

How this was measured: deferred to G2: oversize-file ratio · heuristic · @ deadbeefcafe · maps to the standard: bounded module size (ISO/IEC 25010)

N/A5%N/A

Dependency hygiene

Do you actually know what's inside your dependencies?

Do you know every package you depend on — and whether its license is safe to ship?

We couldn't measure this from the outside — and that's the point.

Why it matters

AI adds packages without a second thought. Some carry licenses that legally force you to open-source your code; some are abandoned or risky. Most teams have no idea what's actually in there. This checks whether your dependencies are inventoried and their licenses resolved — no nasty surprises.

With Shipwright, you get a full list of everything you depend on plus a flag on any license or risk surprise — automatically.

Backed by: OWASP "Vulnerable & Outdated Components."

How this was measured: deferred to G2: lockfile → SBOM licenses · unavailable · maps to the standard: dependency license & risk (OWASP)

What your repo is missing

These controls couldn't be measured from the outside — they're exactly what Shipwright adds. That's why your grade goes up the moment you adopt.

  • Test health
  • Change reconciliation
  • Security
  • Dependency hygiene

Top fixable reasons

  1. Change traceability: 1/2 changes linked to a commit, ADR or test run
Honest ceiling. Heuristic estimate from the outside — it inspects history and structure, it does not verify behaviour. A cold-repo grade cannot certify correctness.

So — what now?

The controls greyed out above aren't things you're doing wrong. They're controls a repo can't prove from the outside. Here are your two next steps.

Understand it

Want to actually understand — and control — AI-built code? The Masterclass explains every one of these controls in plain language, step by step.

Get the Masterclass →

Fix it

Ready to light up the greyed-out controls? Run /shipwright-adopt to bring your existing repo under control — it proposes the setup as a reviewable PR, incremental and reversible.

See how adopt works →
Test inventory:3 test files across 1 framework (pytest) — present, not executed; CI workflow present
Network:local-only (default) — pass --allow-network to enrich via GitHub
